title: US military launches further strikes on Iran after Trump says Tehran ‘will pay’ for killing soldiers – Middle East crisis live
summary: US Central Command says latest attack aimed at degrading Iranian military capabilities after president threatens country over deaths of US troops
body: Democrats have seized on the deaths of three US troops killed in Iranian strikes to urge Donald Trump to urgently reverse his resumption of the war with Iran amid widespread anxiety over climbing casualties. Even as US forces launched a 10th consecutive wave of attacks against Iranian targets, concern over the deaths of American service personnel threatened to complicate moves to escalate the conflict, which has reignited after he last week walked away from a memorandum of understanding agreed with Iran in June. The Pentagon released the names of two soldiers killed in a strike on Muwaffaq Salti air base in Jordan on Friday. They were First Lieutenant Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii, and Private Isabella Gonzales, 19, of Carrollton, Texas. A third member of the armed forces was killed in northern Iraq on Saturday in what US Central Command called a “controlled detonation” of unexploded ordnance. The remains of a possible fourth dead service member were found over the weekend in Jordan. Chris Murphy, a Democratic senator for Connecticut, accused Trump of having “frighteningly lost control of this war”. The British maritime agency says it has received multiple reports that a tanker is saying it has been struck by an unknown projectile in the strait of Hormuz. It occurred eight nautical miles north-east of Limah, Oman, and authorities are investigating, the United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations agency says in a report posted on X. More on this as it comes to light. Kuwait’s air defences are responding to hostile missile and drone attacks launched by Iran, the Kuwaiti army said on Monday. It comes after the US military earlier announced a tenth consecutive night of strikes on Iran. Tehran has been responding with attacks on its Gulf neighbours that host US forces, including Kuwait. Iranian media reported explosions in parts of the country including the port city of Bandar Abbas, after US Central Command announced a new wave of strikes on Iran for the tenth night in a row. The Mehr news agency also reported air defence systems being activated near the Bushehr nuclear power plant. “The strikes are designed to further degrade Iranian military capabilities used to attack commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz,” Centcom said. It came hours after Donald Trump said Iran would pay for killing US soldiers “many times over” after multiple service members ‌were killed in ⁠action over the weekend. The US military on Monday identified two soldiers killed in Jordan while defending against Iranian ballistic missile and drone attacks. An examination of remains is underway to determine whether they belong to a third service member who was reporting missing in that attack. And in Iraq, another US service member died after “a controlled detonation” of an Iranian drone. It brings the total number of US military deaths in the war with Iran, which the US and Israel started in late February, to 17. Earlier, Iran’s president said the country is in a “full-scale war” after the United States expanded its aerial campaign and Tehran struck Washington’s Gulf allies, even as diplomats said talks are ongoing to de-escalate. “The reality is that today the Islamic Republic of Iran is engaged in a full-scale war,” Masoud Pezeshkian, said in a statement, warning Iran might have to endure more strikes. There were glimmers of hope, however, on the diplomatic front, as Iran’s interior minister traveled to Pakistan, a key mediator in the conflict, for talks. Yemen’s Iran-backed Houthi rebels announced a maritime blockade Saudi Arabia after the two sides traded fire last week for the first time in years. Condemning the blockade, Saudi Arabia’s foreign ministry said the kingdom “will take all necessary measures to protect its vessels in accordance with international law”. The Lebanese army began taking charge of security in three southern villages, the United States said, as a deal to secure an Israeli withdrawal from southern Lebanon and the disarmament of Hezbollah faced its first test on the ground. The US state department said operations had started in “pilot zones” in Froun, Srifa and Zawtar al-Gharbiya in accordance with the trilateral framework. However, the mayor of Froun, Hassan Bazzi, told Al Jazeera on Monday that the Lebanese army had not yet begun deploying in Zawtar al-Gharbiya, because Israeli forces are yet to withdraw from the outskirts of the town. “There have been no new developments in the town of Froun. The situation remains unchanged,” said. ⁠Trump also said on Monday that Israeli prime minister Benjamin Netanyahu will not be arrested ⁠when he visits ⁠the United States, after New York City mayor Zohran Mamdani said he was still ⁠considering the move. “Benjamin Netanyahu will not be arrested, in any way, shape, or form, while ⁠in the United States of America,” Trump wrote on social media, without referring to Mamdani’s comments. Hamas named Khalil al-Hayya as its new overall leader, according to a statement from the Palestinian militant ⁠group, succeeding Yahya Sinwar who was killed by the Israeli military in Gaza in October 2024. Hayya, ⁠the group’s exiled Gaza chief and top negotiator, is generally seen as more hardline than ⁠Khaled Meshaal, the head of the militant group’s office in exile and the other frontrunner for the leadership. Jordan said three missiles launched from Iran were intercepted. No injuries or material damage has been reported. Sirens sounded in the capital Amman on Monday evening, AFP reported. Saudi Arabia on Monday condemned an announcement by Yemen’s Houthis of a maritime blockade of the kingdom, as the Iran-backed rebels threatened the top oil exporter’s ability to bypass the strait of Hormuz. In a statement the Saudi foreign ministry said it “expresses the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia’s strongest condemnation of the statements made by the so-called military spokesman of the terrorist Houthi militia ... imposing a maritime blockade on the Kingdom.” The kingdom, it said, “will take all necessary measures to protect its vessels in accordance with international law.” It added that Riyadh “continues to support the brotherly Yemeni people and their legitimate government”. Iranian state TV is reporting explosions in the port city of Bandar Abbas. And the Mehr news agency is reporting air defence systems being activated near the Bushehr nuclear power plant. The US military began a fresh round of strikes against Iran about 20 minutes ago, according to Centcom. “The strikes are designed to further degrade Iranian military capabilities used to attack commercial shipping in the Strait of Hormuz,” it said in a statement. The Lebanese army began taking charge of security in three southern villages on Monday, the United States said, as a deal to secure an Israeli withdrawal from southern Lebanon and the disarmament of Hezbollah faced its first test on the ground. The US state department said operations had started in “pilot zones” in Froun, Srifa and Zawtar al-Gharbiya “in accordance with the Trilateral Framework and under the auspices of the Military Coordination Group for Lebanon.” Spokesman Tommy Pigott described the step as a direct outcome of talks held between Israeli and Lebanese delegations in Rome last week. The framework, signed by Lebanon, Israel and the US on 26 June, envisions a phased Israeli withdrawal from positions in southern Lebanon. In exchange, the Lebanese Armed Forces are to assume sole security control of vacated areas and certify that they have been cleared of Hezbollah weapons and infrastructure. Hezbollah has rejected the framework, with the Iran-backed group insisting any arrangement in Lebanon be linked to wider negotiations between Washington and Tehran. It has not said whether it will resist implementation in the designated areas. The agreement does not set a timetable for Israel’s withdrawal. Israeli officials have also vowed that their forces will remain in a “security zone” six miles deep along the frontier for as long as Hezbollah remains armed. The Lebanese military said on Monday that troops “continue to carry out their missions in the southern regions, including the towns of Froun and Srifa.” “In parallel, coordination is underway with the Military Coordination Group for Lebanon (MCG4L) to begin deployment in the town of Zawtar al-Gharbiya,” awaiting Israel’s withdrawal from there, the army added. The mayor of Froun, Hassan Bazzi, told Al Jazeera on Monday that the Lebanese army has not yet begun deploying in Zawtar al-Gharbiya, because Israeli forces are yet to withdraw from the outskirts of the town. “There have been no new developments in the town of Froun. The situation remains unchanged,” said. Lebanese president Joseph Aoun is set to meet with Donald Trump in Washington on Tuesday. An ⁠explosive-laden drone was ⁠shot ⁠down near Iraq’s al-Harir air ⁠base which houses ⁠US forces, ‌security sources told ‌Reuters. There were no immediate reports of ‌casualties or damage from the incident which was ⁠reported east of Erbil. Here is more from Jordan’s army on intercepting the missiles coming from Iran. “The air defence system intercepted and downed tonight three missiles coming from Iranian territory that entered Jordanian airspace and targeted the kingdom’s territory,” the army said in a statement, adding it “did not lead to any injuries or material damage”. Jordan’s armed forces said three missiles launched from Iran have been intercepted, state TV is reporting. No injuries or material damage has been reported. Sirens sounded in the capital Amman on Monday evening, AFP reported. The mayor of the Lebanese town of Froun, Hassan Bazzi, said the Lebanese army has not begun deploying troops in the town of Zawtar al-Gharbiya because Israeli forces are yet to withdraw from the outskirts of the town. He told Al Jazeera: “This is just talk. There have been no new developments in the town of Froun. The situation remains unchanged.” Sirens sounded in the Jordanian city of Amman…

title: United Airlines says it will ‘look into’ worker’s threat to call ICE in viral video
summary: United staffer told customer ‘you don’t act like at a citizen’ during incident over wait time at San Francisco airport
body: United Airlines is “looking into” a viral video showing a United Airlines employee threatening to call Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) on Mexican American man at San Francisco international Airport last week. The scene erupted after Julio Varela of San Ramon grew upset at United Airlines staff for taking a long time to correct an error his family made when entering his daughter’s name to buy a ticket for flight to Canada, according to NBC News. After three hours of waiting and a sour exchange with a United Airlines staffer, Varela pulled out his phone and demanded the woman to identify her self. “I’m asking for your name so I can report you,” Varela said in a video that has attracted wide attention. The United staffer responded by pulling out her own phone and recording him. “Maybe we should call ICE on you,” the woman replied. “You don’t act like a citizen.” Ramon, who is a naturalized citizen, grew still more angry after that comment. “Call ICE on me, lady,” the man says. The two then trade volleys, with Varela repeatedly saying “say it again” as she responds: “Get out my face.” “United Airlines has a racist customer service lady!” Varela yells. “She’s lazy as hell!” Another United Airlines staffer can be seen sighing and rolling her eyes. “We’re looking into the interaction in this video, but don’t have anything additional to share at this time,” the airline wrote in an emailed statement. Varela expressed concern about possibly being detained by ICE upon his return to the United States from Montreal, despite being a US citizen. But nothing happened when he returned, according to NBC News.

title: Trump exploring additional tariffs on Canada for wildfire smoke impacts in US, White House officials says – US politics live
summary: New 50% tariffs exclude energy products, fish and critical minerals, but include goods that had previously been protected by the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement
body: Jamieson Greer, the US trade representative, said in a statement that the new 50% tariffs on imported Canadian goods imposed on Monday by Donald Trump, under Section 338 of the Tariff Act of 1930, was entirely justified. “While the Administration continues to secure fair and reciprocal trade deals with our trading partners, Canada, unlike other partners and allies, continues to retaliate against the United States for its efforts to rebalance trade and protect U.S. industry in national-security sensitive sectors,” Greer said. “Specifically, Canada has taken U.S. alcohol products off Canadian shelves, given better market access to dairy products from the European Union, and has put a cap on U.S. vehicle exports to Canada from companies reshoring to the United States.” What Greer did not say is that Canada’s actions, including local bans on selling US alcohol products in some Canadian provinces, were taken in response to tariffs imposed by Trump in the first place, and his threats to annex the country. Democrats have seized on the deaths of three US troops killed in Iranian strikes to urge Donald Trump to urgently reverse his resumption of the war with Iran amid widespread anxiety over climbing casualties. Even as US forces launched a 10th consecutive wave of attacks against Iranian targets, concern over the deaths of American service personnel threatened to complicate moves to escalate the conflict, which has reignited after he last week walked away from a memorandum of understanding agreed with Iran in June. The Pentagon released the names of two soldiers killed in a strike on Muwaffaq Salti air base in Jordan on Friday. They were First Lieutenant Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii, and Private Isabella Gonzales, 19, of Carrollton, Texas. A third member of the armed forces was killed in northern Iraq on Saturday in what US Central Command called a “controlled detonation” of unexploded ordnance. The remains of a possible fourth dead service member were found over the weekend in Jordan. Chris Murphy, a Democratic senator for Connecticut, accused Trump of having “frighteningly lost control of this war”. “For those of us who pay attention to the hour-to-hour pace of the Iran War, things are getting very dark very quickly,” he wrote on social media. “The scale of attacks – and threats of escalation – are increasing rapidly.” “The reckless and costly war of choice in Iran has been a disaster for the American people,” wrote Hakeem Jeffries, the Democratic leader in the House of Representatives. “May God continue to watch over our troops who have needlessly been put into harm’s way.” Mark Warner, the vice chair of the Senate intelligence committee and a Democrat from Virginia, said there were “hundreds of seriously wounded” US personnel, and added it was “remarkable” that casualty figures had not been higher. As the global economy reels from the war he started with Iran in February, Donald Trump stepped up his trade war with Canada on Monday, signing three proclamations to impose 50% tariffs on most imported Canadian goods, citing a provision of a 1930 trade law. Trump claimed that the tariffs on one of the most important US trade partners, which risk higher inflation in the US, were justified by what he claimed was Canada’s unfair discrimination against American cars, alcohol and dairy products. A Trump administration official told the Associated Press that Canada was one of the only nations other than China that retaliated against Trump’s previous tariffs and must be held accountable. Several Democratic lawmakers last year proposed repealing the section of the 1930 Tariff Act used by Trump, fearing that he could use it to destabilize the economy after many of his tariffs were ruled illegal by the US supreme court. The new 50% tariffs exclude energy products, fish and critical minerals, but include goods that had previously been protected from import taxes by the United States-Mexico-Canada Agreement, or USMCA. That 2020 trade pact recently expired and triggered a new set of negotiations that could run until 2036. There could be more tariffs to come. The official who briefed reporters on the proclamations said that Trump had also asked his aides look into additional tariffs on Canada because its wildfires hurt air quality in the US. At the World Cup final on Sunday, Trump congratulated players on stage with Canada’s prime minister Mark Carney, a tournament co-host, who has pushed back on Trump’s tariffs and threats to annex Canada. Trump claims in the proclamations that Canada discriminates against American autos, alcohol and cheese relative to other nations, but his argument rests in large part on retaliatory actions taken by Canada after he imposed a series of tariffs on Canada last year. Trump’s proclamation on alcohol notes that Canadian provinces and territories halted the purchase and retailing of American alcoholic beverages last year, without noting that action was in response to Trump’s tariffs and taunts about making Canada the 51st US state. Donald Trump has said Benjamin Netanyahu, the Israeli prime minister, “will not be arrested” if he visits the US. His comments came days after Zohran Mamdani, the New York City mayor, said he was exploring whether his administration could arrest Netanyahu if he visits the city during the UN general assembly in September. In a Truth Social post on Monday, Trump said Netanyahu, who has been the subject of an international criminal court arrest warrant since November 2024 over alleged war crimes in Gaza, “will not be arrested, in any way, shape, or form while in the United States of America”. Trump added that Netanyahu is “fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran” and said “the only ones that should be arrested are the people that led Iran into this unprecedented spiral of death and destruction”. Speaking to the New York Times on Saturday, Mamdani had said: “I believe that prime minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” where the international criminal court is based, adding: “He’s a war criminal who has been charged by the international criminal court, and what you will find is that is an opinion that is held by many, purely because of what his actions have wrought over these past many years.” Mamdani, who has described Israel’s war on Gaza as a genocide, also said he is in “an active conversation” with New York City’s law department about his legal authority to direct the New York police to detain Netanyahu. “Whatever the law allows me to do in New York City, that’s what we will do,” he said, adding: “But we won’t be writing our own laws to that end.” Nancy Mace, a Republican congresswoman from South Carolina, announced on Monday that she is not entering the race to replace the late senator Lindsey Graham in the snap Republican primary next month. In a social-media video posted after the late senator’s sister, Darline Graham, now South Carolina’s interim senator, announced that she is running for the Republican nomination, Mace said that she plans to focus instead on obtaining justice for survivors of sexual abuse, including herself. Mace, who was raped as a teenager, broke with Donald Trump to vote for the release of the Epstein files, and last year accused her ex-fiancé of sexual assault in a speech on the House floor, said: “I’m not chasing another title. I’m chasing justice. Justice for me, justice for other women, and justice for every little girl who was told to stay silent.” The congresswoman, who is leaving office at the end of her current term, and finished fifth in the Republican primary for South Carolina governor last month, also promised to continue her provocative presence on social media, where she is known for her virulent anti-trans and anti-Muslim comments. “Serving South Carolina has been the honor of my life and I will continue to voice our values everywhere I can,” Mace said. “I’m even going to shitpost from time to time because someone has to call out the dumbasses because if we don’t, we as a country will be gone.” Donald Trump signed an executive order on Monday that makes it harder for US defense contractors to obtain waivers that allow them to buy critical minerals from an “unreliable foreign supplier”, as part of an ongoing effort to decrease the importance of China to US defense supply chains. The new rules mean defense contractors like Lockheed Martin and Boeing seeking waivers to allow them to buy from a Chinese supplier will need to prove that they searched for alternatives, ⁠explain where their materials come from and lay out a plan to move away from a foreign-owned source. Currently waivers can be obtained by showing that a Chinese supplier is the easiest or cheapest option. “No more: ‘we tried ‌nothing and we’re out of options’,” the White House trade adviser and anti-China hawk Peter Navarro said at a briefing on the order, Reuters reports. Many critical ‌minerals and processed materials used in missiles, aircraft and other USA military systems still rely on Chinese suppliers, leaving companies caught between demands to move away from Beijing and the need to keep weapons flowing to US forces and allies. Darline Graham, the South Carolina interim senator and sister of the late Lindsey Graham, said she plans to run for a full Senate term. In an interview with Sean Hannity that will air in full on Monday, Graham – who was appointed by Governor Henry McMaster to finish the remainder of her brother’s term – said she had “made a decision”. “I’m in,” she told the Fox News anchor. Graham, 62, made history as the first woman to represent South Carolina in the Senate when she was sworn in last week. Her brother, Lindsey died earlier this month, aged 71, after what his office called a “brief and sudden illness”. The chief medical examiner then preliminarily ruled that he died of aortic dissection due to cardiovascular disease. On Friday, Trump said he had encouraged Darline Graham to run for a full six-year term representing South…

title: Healey made chancellor, Miliband named foreign secretary and Yvette Cooper becomes health secretary as Burnham picks cabinet – UK politics as it happened
summary: Rayner is back as housing secretary, while Mahmood and McFadden remain in their existing posts
body: Andy Burnham has announced his final cohort of Cabinet ministers. Stephen Kinnock is the new Secretary of State for Wales, while Sir Chris Bryant MP is Secretary of State for Northern Ireland, Downing Street said. Ellie Reeves is the new Attorney General, and she will attend Cabinet. Emma Reynolds is the new Chief Secretary to the Treasury. She will attend Cabinet. Kanishka Narayan will be a minister of state across the Cabinet Office and the Department for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport. He will attend Cabinet. Hamish Falconer is a minister across both the Cabinet Office and Foreign Office. He will attend Cabinet. Heidi Alexander remains as Transport Secretary and Douglas Alexander remains as Scotland Secretary. Sir Alan Campbell MP will remain as Leader of the House of Commons. Baroness Smith of Basildon remains as Leader of the House of Lords. Matthew Pennycook MP will remain as a minister in the Ministry of Housing, Communities and Local Government. He will attend Cabinet. John Healey has been named chancellor in Andy Burnham’s first Cabinet appointment. Healey resigned as defence secretary last month in protest at Keir Starmer’s defence investment plan. Yvette Cooper has been appointed health secretary while Shabana Mahmood keeps her job as home secretary. Ed Miliband has been named foreign secretary while Wes Streeting has been appointed defence secretary. Angela Rayner said she is “delighted” to be reappointed housing secretary, adding on X: “Andy Burnham’s vision of good growth in every postcode demands we build on the foundations we have already laid to bring the change we have promised.” Louise Haigh has been named as Andy Burnham’s first secretary of state, chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster and minister for the Cabinet Office. Alex Norris is the new justice secretary and Dame Angela Eagle is the new environment secretary, Downing Street said. Miatta Fahnbulleh has been appointed the energy secretary. Pat McFadden will remain in his role as work and pensions secretary and Lisa Nandy will remain as culture secretary. The ministers to lose their jobs included Rachel Reeves, David Lammy, Darren Jones, Steve Reed, Peter Kyle, Richard Hermer, Liz Kendall, Jo Stevens and Hilary Benn. In his first speech outside Downing Street after being named prime minster, Andy Burnham said his first instruction will be to end rough sleeping. He said later this year, he will bring forward a new plan for Britain, “a 10-year plan”. In his final speech as PM, Keir Starmer said he’s leaving No 10 with “good grace” and a “smile”. He finished by saying Andy Burnham had his “full support”. Responding to the announcement that Alex Norris has been appointed as justice secretary, Katie Kempen, CEO of charity Victim Support, said: “With millions of victims and survivors paying the price for a broken criminal justice system, the new Justice Secretary inherits a formidable challenge from day one. “Strengthening support for victims and delivering their rights must be Alex Norris’s top priority. Shamefully, victims continue to routinely miss out on their basic rights while legislation designed to enforce them is gathering dust. The MoJ must act now to rectify this. “Meanwhile, the abolition of Police and Crime Commissioners presents a once-in-a-generation opportunity to improve how victims’ services are funded and delivered. The Justice Secretary must grasp this chance to ensure every victim can access independent, specialist support, wherever they live.” Alex Norris is the new justice secretary and Dame Angela Eagle is the new environment secretary, Downing Street said. Miatta Fahnbulleh has been appointed the energy secretary. Wes Streeting has been appointed defence secretary. Angela Rayner said she is “delighted” to be reappointed housing secretary, adding on X: “Andy Burnham’s vision of good growth in every postcode demands we build on the foundations we have already laid to bring the change we have promised.” Charity Shelter has given its reaction to the appointment of Angela Rayner as housing secretary. Sarah Elliott, its chief executive, said: “The Prime Minister has given Angela Rayner a clear mandate to roll out a council housing revolution and put an end to rough sleeping – now it’s her time to deliver. “Having grown up in social housing, Rayner knows better than anyone that an affordable, decent home is the foundation for everything, giving people the building blocks they need to get on in life and setting children up to thrive. “The new Secretary of State has a strong track record advocating for social housing - in choosing her the Prime Minister again shows he is serious about tackling the housing emergency. With child homelessness at a record high, Rayner must now pull every lever to get councils building again and deliver a new generation of social rent homes.” Health minister Stephen Kinnock, the son of former Labour leader Neil Kinnock, has entered Downing Street. Scotland Secretary Douglas Alexander has also walked into a busy No 10. Lisa Nandy will remain as the culture secretary, Downing Street said. Nandy told reporters she was “very” happy with her job as she left Downing Street smiling on Monday evening. Prime minister Andy Burnham has spoken to French president Emmanuel Macron. A Downing Street spokeswoman said: “The Prime Minister began by setting out how he is looking forward to building on the strong UK-France relationship to deliver for people on both sides of the Channel. “Turning to Ukraine, the two leaders discussed the need to work together to continue supporting Ukraine as it resists Russia’s full-scale invasion, including through co-chairing the Coalition of the Willing. “On the Middle East, the two leaders agreed the need to continue efforts towards deescalation and to reopen the Strait of Hormuz. “They also discussed continuing the UK and France’s close co-operation on energy and defence, and the Prime Minister paid tribute to President Macron for his efforts to bring the Bayeux Tapestry to the UK, which underlined the UK and France’s enduring friendship. “The two leaders look forward to speaking again and the Prime Minister welcoming President Macron to the United Kingdom at the earliest opportunity.” On Politics Weekly, Pippa Crerar and Kiran Stacey look at how Andy Burnham will try to do things differently and who will be helping him in the cabinet. Several other ministers and government figures have entered Downing Street in the past hour, including trade minister Chris Bryant, leader of the House of Lords Angela Smith, solicitor general Ellie Reeves, environment secretary Emma Reynolds and housing minister Matthew Pennycook. Bridget Phillipson, who has been replaced as education secretary by Lucy Powell, will remain a cabinet member as minister for women and equalities. Angela Rayner has been reappointed as the secretary of state for housing, communities and local government. She quit the role and her position as deputy prime minister last September after a row over stamp duty. Lucy Powell is the new education secretary, replacing her Labour deputy leadership rival Bridget Phillipson. Jonathan Reynolds has moved from chief whip to business secretary, replacing Peter Kyle. Anneliese Midgley is the new chief whip. Wes Streeting, the former health secretary, has entered 10 Downing Street. Streeting had been seen as a potential leadership candidate but backed Burnham. He is joined by the Home Office ministers Angela Eagle and Alex Norris, the former housing minister Miatta Fahnbulleh and the transport secretary, Heidi Alexander. The new chancellor John Healey said the government led by Andy Burnham “will meet our commitments on defence to our international allies”. He was asked by broadcasters on Monday night if he would meet a commitment to spend 3% of national economic output on defence by 2030. Healey expressed frustration that Sir Keir Starmer had not set out a clear timetable for the commitment when he resigned last month as defence secretary. Asked if welfare cuts or new taxes were on the cards to meet the goal, Healey said: “Fiscal control is the first duty of any chancellor. It is mine. And fiscal credibility is the bedrock for economic stability and for national security. “You heard the Prime Minister this afternoon say, in this more dangerous world, we will meet our commitments on defence to our international allies.” Andy Burnham has said his arrival in power will “bring back hope” to Britain as he launched his premiership with the surprise appointment of John Healey as chancellor and a promise to repair the damage caused to communities during the Thatcher era. The new UK prime minister acknowledged people were “fed up” with politics as he addressed the country after taking over from Keir Starmer in Downing Street, saying the government needed to “do better” to deliver real change. In his speech outside No 10, he said the government would announce “some breathing space” on Tuesday for people on the cost of living, with plans to cut energy bills and bus fares expected as part of a fully funded package. Burnham later told reporters that he would look at “any flexibility” within the government’s existing fiscal rules to help borrow billions more to invest in infrastructure – but would take a “very prudent” approach to the economy. UK 10-year gilt prices dropped after his comments, with the markets already on the edge over the risks of higher borrowing and increased debt. But they had closed by the time that Healey, regarded as a safe pair of hands, was announced. The former defence secretary, whose resignation pushed Starmer to the brink, emerged as the compromise candidate after Ed Miliband and Shabana Mahmood were tipped for the role. Miliband takes over as foreign secretary while Mahmood remains at the Home Office. Yvette Cooper said it was a real honour to be appointed Health Secretary by Andy Burnham “27 years after I worked on the first NHS cancer plan as a junior health minist…

title: Briefing wars: how Labour ministers tried, and failed, to get into Burnham’s cabinet
summary: Choice of John Healey for chancellor shocks insiders after weeks of fierce lobbying on behalf of party big hitters
body: For weeks, as ministers jostled, lobbied and positioned themselves for big jobs in an Andy Burnham government, the incoming prime minister remained sphinx-like. He told an interviewer on Monday that he had a black box containing his ministerial picks and major policy announcements, adding that even as he prepared to enter Downing Street: “The black box is still sealed.” And so, when John Healey was appointed chancellor on Monday after intense briefing from allies of Ed Miliband, Shabana Mahmood and Rachel Reeves, it blindsided even those at the top of the Labour party. “We did not see this coming,” said one Labour MP. “But that is probably a sign that it is a good decision.” Officials said the prime minister had chosen the former defence secretary because the two agreed on a series of policy ideas, including sticking to borrowing rules, re-industrialisation, devolution and intervention to help with the cost of living. Healey also has Treasury credentials, having been a junior minister in the department for five years under Gordon Brown, during which time he earned a reputation as a stalwart Brownite. Healey’s appointment gives an indication of the prime minister’s thinking in certain key areas, not least defence spending. His resignation as defence secretary last month, in protest at the proposed defence investment plan, was a critical moment in the downfall of Burnham’s predecessor, Keir Starmer. Some Starmer allies believe that resignation sapped the former prime minister of his will to fight Burnham for the premiership. Some even think Healey did a deal to become Burnham’s chancellor before announcing his resignation from Starmer’s government. Healey will almost certainly use his new position to increase defence spending. One way to do so might be to issue “defence bonds” – new borrowing allocated only for the military – which Healey advocated in government and now has a chance to enact. While the chancellor’s allies have been pushing his case in recent weeks, they have done so less vociferously than others. Healey himself told friends he was looking forward to having a break from government to spend time with his wife. Friends of three other big hitters – Ed Miliband, Shabana Mahmood and Rachel Reeves had been more active in Labour circles and in the press. Weeks before Burnham was elected as Makerfield MP, some in the Labour party thought Reeves was positioning herself to stay in post as chancellor. Her proposal to freeze private sector rents – something that had not previously been mentioned as a government policy and was quickly slapped down by Starmer and other senior ministers – was seen as deliberate signal to the more interventionist Burnham. Katie Martin, one of her most senior advisers, was calling business lobbyists and executives asking them to press her case, either publicly or privately. Supportive MPs were also rallying round. One told the Guardian: “I am concerned that we may lose everything if a new leader sacrifices the chancellor for promises and new alliances they are currently forging with MPs who fancy the job for themselves. The biggest fear for the bond markets and the unions is Ed Miliband.” In the end, none of this pressure was enough. Reeves resigned from the government on Monday having turned down the offer of another big appointment – reported to be that of defence secretary. Miliband’s allies also spent time pressing his case. Friends responded angrily to suggestions that he would not be business-friendly, arguing that his efforts to increase funding on green infrastructure had been pro-growth and pro-industrialisation. Several union leaders gave him their public endorsement – an unprecedented move for a potential cabinet pick. “We need a chancellor who will rewire the economy and properly invest to improve the lives of the majority,” said the Unison leader, Andrea Egan. “Of those reported to be in the running, only Ed Miliband could enact the kinds of policies trade unions and our members urgently need.” Many of those closest to Burnham also indicated they thought he should be the pick. One said: “We need someone who knows the Treasury, can push back on what civil servants naturally want to do and has a proven track record of achieving things in government. That has to be Ed.” Speaking soon after the Makerfield byelection, another said choosing Miliband as chancellor was a decision the incoming prime minister should “spend political capital on”. Others, however, were always sceptical. One Burnham ally described Miliband’s previous leadership of his party as having “screwed up the Labour party for a decade”. Another accused his allies of “living in cloud cuckoo land”. The heavy lobbying from senior figures in business and the markets against Miliband’s candidacy seemed to count against him however. With the Iran war threatening another bout of inflation and borrowing costs having moved up in recent weeks, even his former supporters expressed doubt in recent days. “It might just be time for a fiscal hawk,” said one MP who had previously advocated on his behalf. It was against this rancorous backdrop that those close to Mahmood thought she might have a chance to take over at the Treasury. She had told friends she wanted to remain as home secretary to oversee the contentious changes to the asylum and immigration that she launched earlier this year. But as the Labour party became more divided over who should be chancellor, her allies began mentioning her as a possible pick, despite having said little about economic policy in the past. Those who do know her economic views say she would have been fiscally dry – indicating she would be resistant to borrowing much more – but coupled with a commitment to re-industrialisation that echoed Burnham’s own. The prime minister is known to be an admirer of Mahmood, indicating during the Makerfield campaign that he backed her migration reforms despite significant opposition in the parliamentary Labour party. Her case was being taken so seriously among Burnham’s supporters that one of them told the Financial Times just days ago: “Shabana is nailed down as chancellor. That’s definitely happening.” In the end, she will be allowed to continue in post, as she always said she wanted, in a clear sign there will be no major watering down of her proposals. It was not just the Treasury, however, that became the subject of heavy lobbying from cabinet ministers uncertain of their fate. Steve Reed, the outgoing housing and communities secretary, gave interviews and speeches in his final weeks in office stressing his commitment to devolution – one of Burnham’s main policy priorities. Reed’s allies expressed frustration that he was not getting credit for what the government had already done to push power out of Whitehall – but it was not enough to save his job and he left the government on Monday. Several other ministers were also privately candid about what jobs they would ideally like to do. More than one talked about their expertise in the technology sector – though in vain, given Burnham is expected to scrap the technology department altogether. Some in international roles were keen to stress their domestic policy credentials, given that is likely to be where the prime minister puts much of his focus. However, those close to the prime minister say the lobbying campaigns waged by ministers’ proxies may have backfired. “Andy hates people briefing themselves into jobs,” said one aide. “Anyone doing so is just as likely to find themselves out of government.”

title: ‘It needs to end now’: Democrats urge Trump to halt Iran war after US troop deaths
summary: President accused of having ‘lost control of this war’ as deaths of trio in Iranian military strikes fuel rising concern
body: Democrats have seized on the deaths of three US troops killed in Iranian strikes to urge Donald Trump to urgently reverse his resumption of the war with Iran amid widespread anxiety over climbing casualties. Even as US forces launched a 10th consecutive wave of attacks against Iranian targets, concern over the deaths of American service personnel threatened to complicate moves to escalate the conflict, which has reignited after he last week walked away from a memorandum of understanding agreed with Iran in June. The Pentagon released the names of two soldiers killed in a strike on Muwaffaq Salti air base in Jordan on Friday. They were First Lieutenant Tyler James Feehan, 25, of Ewa Beach, Hawaii, and Private Isabella Gonzales, 19, of Carrollton, Texas. A third member of the armed forces was killed in northern Iraq on Saturday in what US Central Command called a “controlled detonation” of unexploded ordnance. The remains of a possible fourth dead service member were found over the weekend in Jordan. Chris Murphy, a Democratic senator for Connecticut, accused Trump of having “frighteningly lost control of this war”. “For those of us who pay attention to the hour-to-hour pace of the Iran War, things are getting very dark very quickly,” he wrote on social media. “The scale of attacks – and threats of escalation – are increasing rapidly.” The defense secretary, Pete Hegseth, declared that the US would not be deterred by the casualties, writing: “Godspeed, heroes. Their sacrifice only stiffens our resolve.” Iran has vowed to carry out retaliatory strikes against US Gulf allies, which it has branded “collaborators” for providing logistical facilities for strikes on Iranian territory. In a brief telephone conversation with NewsNation, Trump called the deaths “a very sad thing” and said the service members had died “in service of our country”. But he faced a chorus of calls from Democrats to step back from his belligerent posture even as Republicans remained supportive. “The reckless and costly war of choice in Iran has been a disaster for the American people,” wrote Hakeem Jeffries, the Democratic leader in the House of Representatives. “May God continue to watch over our troops who have needlessly been put into harm’s way.” Mark Warner, the vice chair of the Senate intelligence committee and a Democrat from Virginia, said there were “hundreds of seriously wounded” US personnel, and added it was “remarkable” that casualty figures had not been higher. “This is what happens when you start a war of choice. And as a member of the Gang of Eight, I can say unequivocally there was no imminent threat from Iran,” he told CBS’s Face the Nation. Chris Van Hollen, a Democratic senator from Maryland who has introduced war powers resolutions aimed at ending the Iran campaign unless it receives congressional approval, posted: “This senseless war should never have been started and it needs to end NOW.” Jeanne Shaheen, the party’s ranking members of the Senate foreign relations committee, called for a return to diplomacy. “The President must negotiate an end to this disastrous war,” she wrote. And Ted Lieu, a California representative who has also vocally advocated reining in Trump through a war powers resolution, wrote: “We need Republicans to join us in passing the War Powers Resolution NOW.” The New York Times reported that Iranian forces carried out three strikes against US military targets in Jordan last week before Friday’s deadly attacks that were not disclosed by the Pentagon. The newspaper said the Pentagon also did not reveal “dozens” of casualties and damage inflicted by the earlier strikes. The latest casualties brought the number of US service members killed since the start of the war on 28 February to at least 17. More than 1,700 Iranian civilians are estimated to have been killed. The House and Senate have both passed war powers resolutions – requiring the president to ask for congressional approval after 60 days of hostilities – with the aim of restraining Trump. But the White House has contended that there has yet to be 60 days of continuous fighting because the clock stopped after an initial ceasefire in April, which held, shakily, for several weeks. Republicans, meanwhile, have remained publicly supportive of Trump – despite poll figures showing support for the war and the president’s conduct of it low and sliding. A Washington Post-Ipsos survey showed approval for Trump’s handling of the conflict at 29% – lower than the 33% backing his stewardship of the economy, which has been buffeted by soaring fuel prices caused by the war. A statement from New Mexico Republicans mourned the fresh casualties but accused Democrats of trying to exploit them for political reasons. “No matter how much Democrats try to politicize this, President Trump’s actions in Iran have made our country safer from a terrorist regime that has inflicted death and carnage on millions of innocent people around the world, including Americans,” the party said in a statement. James Comer, a representative from Kentucky and one of Trump’s staunchest supporters on Capitol Hill, told Fox News that a military response to the service members’ deaths was essential. “I don’t think anyone would prefer that we have any type of military conflict in Iran, but that’s the world that we live in,” he said. “We have to respond, and we have to respond swiftly and with full force, and that’s what President Trump’s doing.” Riley Moore, a West Virginia Republican, told Fox that the “precision” of the strikes on the Jordanian base suggested Iran was receiving targeting information from Russia or China. He called on the US’s Gulf allies on the Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) to help. “Where do we go from here is also looking at how we’re going to work with those GCC partners in the region because this could tip over … into a regional destabilizing conflict,” he said.

title: Nicaragua’s president says country will not hold any more elections
summary: Daniel Ortega, the 80-year-old authoritarian leader, says move will ‘build a wall’ against the opposition
body: Nicaragua’s long-serving president, Daniel Ortega, has said the country will hold no further elections to prevent the opposition from coming to power. The 80-year-old authoritarian leader, who has been in office since 2007, also said he would work with the congress he controls to pass new laws that would “build a wall” against the opposition. Ortega made the announcement on Sunday during the official ceremony marking the 47th anniversary of the 1979 Sandinista revolution, in which he helped overthrow the brutal dictatorship of Anastasio Somoza. Although his administration and that of his wife and “co-president”, Rosario Murillo, had already been marked by the steady erosion of democracy in the Central American country, many are describing his announcement as a significant rhetorical escalation. “With this decision, Ortega and Murillo have sealed the country’s transition to a full-fledged family dictatorship,” said Tiziano Breda, Latin America senior analyst at the NGO Armed Conflict Location and Event Data. During a speech in front of thousands of state employees who, according to local media, had been coerced into attending, Ortega said: “There will be no more elections here so that they [the opposition parties] can try to seize the government, seize power.” The president, who had not appeared in public for 61 days, added: “We will work with the National Assembly and the relevant institutions on laws, because we need laws that build a wall, a barrier, against the coup plotters and the traitors who sell out their country.” The elections were due to take place next year, but Ortega gave no details on whether they would be cancelled or whether the opposition would be barred from taking part – something that has already happened in practice. During the 2021 campaign, his regime outlawed parties and jailed all opposition presidential hopefuls. Breda said: “Ortega and Murillo are evidently afraid of the idea that the slightest political opening could create the conditions for dissent to manifest and threaten their grip on power, possibly suggesting that their already feeble domestic support is further falling. Therefore, instead of staging a rigged election, they have opted to eliminate electoral competition altogether.” Last year, Ortega pushed through a series of constitutional reforms that included extending the presidential term from five to six years and promoting his wife, then vice-president, to the position of “co-president”. The US refers to their administration as the “Murillo-Ortega dictatorship”. During his speech, the Nicaraguan president criticised the US over the January operation that abducted the Venezuelan dictator Nicolás Maduro: “With all their power, they stormed the presidential palace, where Nicolás and his wife, Cilia [Flores], were. They killed the entire security detail there – most of them were Cubans – and took them away to imprison them in the US. It is monstrous.” The president said preventing the opposition from returning to power was necessary to protect the country from the “coup plotters” who, he claimed, had been behind the nationwide protests of 2018 – when, according to organisations including the UN, his regime carried out a violent crackdown that killed more than 350 people, wounded hundreds, jailed thousands and forced nearly 1 million Nicaraguans into exile. Recently, the UN, the US, human rights organisations and 30 former Ibero-American presidents have directly blamed Ortega and Murillo for the death of the Indigenous leader and four-term congressman Brooklyn Rivera, 73, who spent nearly three years “unjustly imprisoned” by the regime, the US state department said. According to organisations including Human Rights Watch, there are about 50 political prisoners in the country. The regime has stripped 546 people of their citizenship and shut down more than 5,600 non-governmental organisations, 29 universities and 58 media outlets.

title: By the numbers: the latest ICE and CBP data on arrests, detentions and deportations in the US
summary: The Guardian has reviewed figures from Immigration and Customs Enforcement and Customs and Border Protection since Trump’s inauguration
body: Donald Trump campaigned on a platform of mass deportation. Since he took office, his administration has reshaped immigration enforcement across the country. The Guardian, using data published every two weeks by US Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE), is tracking the number of people the administration has arrested, detained and deported. Notes on the data ICE usually publishes detention statistics for the current fiscal year every two weeks (see footnote). The Guardian has been archiving each release of detention management statistics since January 2025 and received older releases from the Vera Institute for Justice. We calculated the number of people arrested, detained and deported during each reporting period by scraping data from each release and comparing the totals with previous releases. The number of people arrested, in detention, and deported do not have a direct, one to one connection. The immigration legal system is complicated and each figure has to be considered on its own. People can be arrested but not booked into detention, and deported without first being booked into detention. The data for ICE arrests comes from the ICE Initial Book-Ins by Arresting Agency and Month table. Arrest figures may be an undercount because ICE reports only arrests that result in someone entering ICE detention. Detention totals come from the ICE Currently Detained by Criminality and Arresting Agency table. Deportations come from the ICE Removals table. The Guardian US did not visualize the first data release of FY2025. The first release of each year includes carryover data from the previous fiscal year and includes more than two weeks of arrests and deportations. • Update, 5 February 2026: Owing to a change in the frequency that ICE reports the data, small errors were introduced into the annotations of the charts. Before the government shutdown, data was visualized in two-week increments, but after the government shutdown in late 2025, ICE started releasing data inconsistently. The Guardian has now redesigned the charts used to visualize ICE arrests, detentions and deportations to match the changing release schedule.

title: Darline Graham, Lindsey Graham’s sister, to run for full Senate term
summary: Interim senator appointed after Lindsey Graham’s death endorsed by Trump ‘for the Good of our Nation’
body: Darline Graham, the South Carolina interim senator and sister of the late Lindsey Graham, said she plans to run for a full Senate term after Donald Trump endorsed her last week. In an interview with Sean Hannity that will air in full on Monday, Graham – who was appointed by Governor Henry McMaster to finish the remainder of her brother’s term – said she had “made a decision”. “I’m in,” she told the Fox News anchor. Graham, 62, made history as the first woman to represent South Carolina in the Senate when she was sworn in last week. Her brother, Lindsey died earlier this month, aged 71, after what his office called a “brief and sudden illness”. The chief medical examiner preliminarily ruled that he died of aortic dissection due to cardiovascular disease. On Friday, Trump said he had encouraged Darline Graham to run for a full six-year term representing South Carolina. “I asked Darline, for the Good of our Nation, to run for the U.S. Senate in the Special Republican Primary,” he wrote on Truth Social. “Darline, who comes from an absolutely incredible family, has been a WINNER all of her life and, should she accept, has my Complete and Total Endorsement.” Because Lindsey Graham won the Republican nomination last month, the state will now hold a special primary to replace him on the ballot on 11 August.

title: Trump says Benjamin Netanyahu ‘will not be arrested’ if he visits the US
summary: Israeli prime minister is subject of an arrest warrant issued by international criminal court over alleged war crimes in Gaza
body: Donald Trump has said Benjamin Netanyahu, the Israeli prime minister, “will not be arrested” if he visits the US. His comments came days after Zohran Mamdani, the New York City mayor, said he was exploring whether his administration could arrest Netanyahu if he visits the city during the UN general assembly in September. In a Truth Social post on Monday, Trump said Netanyahu, who has been the subject of an international criminal court arrest warrant since November 2024 over alleged war crimes in Gaza, “will not be arrested, in any way, shape, or form while in the United States of America”. Trump added that Netanyahu is “fighting against the Islamic Republic of Iran” and said “the only ones that should be arrested are the people that led Iran into this unprecedented spiral of death and destruction”. Speaking to the New York Times on Saturday, Mamdani had said: “I believe that prime minister Netanyahu belongs in The Hague,” where the international criminal court is based, adding: “He’s a war criminal who has been charged by the international criminal court, and what you will find is that is an opinion that is held by many, purely because of what his actions have wrought over these past many years.” Mamdani, who has described Israel’s war on Gaza as a genocide, also said he is in “an active conversation” with New York City’s law department about his legal authority to direct the New York police to detain Netanyahu. “Whatever the law allows me to do in New York City, that’s what we will do,” he said, adding: “But we won’t be writing our own laws to that end.” Although the US does not recognize the ICC’s jurisdiction and is not a party to the court, Mamdani pledged during his mayoral campaign last September that he would arrest Netanyahu if he traveled to New York. “It is my desire to ensure that this be a city that stands up for international law,” he said. Other Israeli leaders, including Yoav Gallant, the country’s former defense minister, as well as the Hamas military leader Mohammed Deif, have also been the subject of ICC arrest warrants. The court accused the Israeli leaders of war crimes and crimes against humanity in Gaza, including “starvation as a method of warfare, and … murder, persecution, and other inhumane acts”.

title: Small dog missing for weeks rescued while swimming in San Francisco Bay
summary: A team of firefighters saved the pup from the water in a mission that utilized boats, trucks and a drone
body: Firefighters rescued a small dog that had been missing for weeks after finding it swimming into the San Francisco Bay. The dog had gone missing over the Fourth of July weekend, according to the Menlo Park fire protection district. But someone called 911 on 16 July to say they had seen a dog enter the bay near the Dumbarton Bridge in East Palo Alto. The dog “just kept swimming”, according to a Menlo Park fire district Instagram post. The Menlo Park fire protection district mobilized at least two trucks, a boat and an airboat in pursuit of the dog. Rescuers first put eyes on it from the ground with binoculars. Later, they used a drone while pursuing it from the water. The department later released footage of the dog rescue on social media. Both fire and police officers assisted with saving the missing dog, which was photographed getting toweled off by a team of tattooed firefighters. Authorities later turned over to the local Society for the Prevention of Cruelty to Animals (SPCA), which located the pup’s family and returned it. “Dog and family have been reunited,” the Menlo Park fire protection district’s Instagram post reads.

title: US launches 10th round of strikes as Iran says it is in ‘full-scale war’
summary: Diplomats say de-escalation talks continue as Trump vows retaliation over US soldier deaths
body: The US military said it has launched its 10th consecutive round of strikes on Iran to force the reopening of the strait of Hormuz. Earlier on Monday, Iran’s president said the country is in a “full-scale war” after the US expanded its aerial campaign and Tehran struck Washington’s Gulf allies, even as diplomats said talks to de-escalate were continuing. Iranian media reported strikes around Tabriz, believed to hold underground missile bases, as well as Bushehr, home to the country’s only operational civilian nuclear power plant. The US military said it had struck “military command centres, air defence and coastal surveillance sites, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites and communications networks”. Iran launched attacks against Bahrain, which hosts the US navy’s fifth fleet, and Kuwait, in addition to striking tankers in the strait of Hormuz, which carried a fifth of global oil and gas supplies before the war. Bahrain said its air traffic systems were damaged by the strikes, but that traffic was not affected. The Iran-allied Houthis in Yemen also announced a blockade of Saudi ports. “The reality is that today the Islamic Republic of Iran is engaged in a full-scale war,” the Iranian president, Masoud Pezeshkian, said in a statement on Monday, warning it might have to endure more strikes. Meanwhile, US president Donald Trump signalled an escalation in tactics, saying on Monday ⁠that Iran ⁠would “pay” for killing US soldiers “many times over” after multiple service members ‌were killed in action over the weekend. The US military on Monday identified two soldiers killed in Jordan while defending against Iranian ballistic missile and drone attacks. An examination of remains is under way to determine whether they belong to a third service member who was reported missing in that attack. And in Iraq, another US service member died after “a controlled detonation” of an Iranian drone. Since the war began, 17 US service members have been killed. Iran’s health ministry said on Friday that 50 people had been killed and more than 500 injured in the current round of fighting. The latest tit-for-tat attacks came as the region lurched back towards a total war despite an interim peace deal agreed last month. The memorandum of understanding (MoU) to halt attacks and open the strait is more than halfway through a 60-day implementation period that was intended to provide breathing room for diplomacy. Shipping in the strait has slowed to a trickle as a result of the fighting and the price of oil reached its highest in a month, passing $90 (£67) a barrel, exacerbating the global energy crisis. A Houthi blockade of Saudi ports announced on Monday could further strain global energy markets as Saudi Arabia was using the Red Sea route to export millions of barrels of oil and circumvent Iran’s closure of the strait of Hormuz. The group said it was responding to what it called Riyadh’s targeting of Sana’a airport last week after it was bombed by the Saudi-backed Yemen government, and that it would answer a “blockade with a blockade”. But in a sign that talks could still stop the escalating round of conflict, an Iranian official told Reuters that mediators gave Iran a proposal for a 10-day-ceasefire to find ways to revive the MoU. The proposal follows a statement by Iran that diplomacy with the US was continuing despite the fighting, without giving details on the content of the discussions. Iran’s interior minister was expected to travel to Islamabad on Monday, Agence France-Presse reported. Hours earlier, the US secretary of state, Marco Rubio, said the US was still open to negotiations with Iran, but that “it has to be real”. “We’re going to continue to respond. If the door opens to diplomacy – if the guys that want to do something productive for Iran win and take control of that system, or take control of the negotiations – that’ll be a very positive development,” Rubio said. Mediators including Qatar and Pakistan have continued to speak to both sides during the current round of escalation in an attempt to bring the two sides back to the negotiating table. As the strikes began early on Monday, a ship caught fire in the strait of Hormuz near the coastline of Oman in the southern corridor the US military has encouraged ships to use to avoid Iran’s control. The crew safely abandoned the vessel, while the ship was recovered by a tug boat, the United Kingdom Maritime Trade Operations agency said. Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) claimed two oil tankers were blown up after attempting to transit through the southern route, but there was no independent confirmation. The IRGC also claimed to have launched a “surprise attack” on an enemy command centre in the al-Tanf region of Syria, in a statement reported by the state news agency IRNA. After Iran targeted sites in Jordan on Sunday, Israel warned of the danger of the strikes spilling across its borders, reigniting a direct regional conflict that had been restrained even as tensions between the Trump administration and Iran have soared again. “If Iran fires missiles at Israel, we will attack them with full force,” said the Israeli defence minister, Israel Katz, on Sunday. The US is expected to send dozens of additional refuelling planes to Israel as part of a reinforcement of its force posture in the region, a US official told Reuters. Trump has threatened to target Iran’s power stations and bridges to try to compel Tehran to loosen its hold on the strait of Hormuz. The US energy secretary, Chris Wright, defended the Trump administration’s strategy for the war, but argued that the mission had since shifted toward ensuring oil shipments could move through the strait. “We did a pause to try to get an agreement with the Iranian people to allow oil to flow without them attacking it,” Wright said on Sunday. “They’ve proven unwilling to do that, and we’ve pivoted. Now what we’re doing is assuring that that oil and gas and other products can flow through the strait of Hormuz with or without Iranian cooperation.” The US also announced that the “pilot zone” scheme in Lebanon had begun in three villages in the south, after peace talks in Rome between the governments of Lebanon and Israel. Under the programme, Israel will withdraw soldiers from areas it occupies in south Lebanon, to be replaced by the Lebanese army, which will be tasked with dismantling Hezbollah infrastructure in the areas. The US state department spokesperson Thomas Pigott said on Monday that the scheme started in the villages of Froun, Srifa and Zawtar al-Gharbiya. The Associated Press contributed reporting

title: Andy Burnham’s first Labour cabinet: who’s in and who’s out?
summary: From the Treasury to the Home Office, we look at who will head up key departments in new PM’s government
body: Andy Burnham has appointed his first cabinet, with the new prime minister rewarding those who have been loyal to his takeover efforts. He had promised to pick his top team from all wings of the party in an effort to show he is trying to run a more inclusive administration. But at the same time, his decision to have a brutal clearout of Starmer allies resulted in many considered to be on the right of the party or close to the former prime minister losing their jobs. These are the key announcements so far: Who is in? John Healey Chancellor The former defence secretary who quit the government over a lack of funding for the defence investment plan is the surprise appointment to the Treasury. Healey, 66, is a steady and experienced force, having been an MP since 1997, and serving as a Treasury minister under Tony Blair. The job had been first expected to go to Ed Miliband and then it was mooted for Shabana Mahmood. But one advantage of Healey is his longstanding history on Labour frontbenches for every faction of the party. He was a minister in the Blair-Brown years and a loyal member of the shadow cabinets of Miliband, Jeremy Corbyn and Starmer. Healey grew up in Wakefield, studied political science at the University of Cambridge, and was a journalist, campaigns director at the Trades Union Congress and a disability rights campaigner before entering parliament. Unshowy and not part of the usual Westminster dramas or scandals, it was a shock when he resigned his role under Starmer, in what would be one of the fatal blows for his premiership. In the job, he will be expected to push for a fuller funding settlement for the Ministry of Defence, which was denied to him under the Treasury of Rachel Reeves. His other major shared interest with Burnham is reindustrialisation. His X bio describes him as “Husband and Dad. Proud Yorkshireman. Labour MP for Rawmarsh and Conisbrough and former secretary of state for defence”. He can now add chancellor to that list. Shabana Mahmood The home secretary Mahmood will keep her role in Burnham’s cabinet after being appointed home secretary by Keir Starmer last year and charged with driving through controversial immigration policies. She is considered to be on the Blue Labour right of the party, which is socially conservative and tough on crime. She grew up in Small Heath in inner-city Birmingham in the 1980s and 90s, where her father kept a cricket bat behind the till of the family shop to fend off would-be robbers. After going to the University of Oxford to study law, she worked as a barrister. The 45-year-old is an MP in Birmingham and ran Labour’s election campaign in 2024, before a stint as justice secretary overseeing an early release programme to ease overcrowding. She is viewed as steely and decisive. A cabinet colleague told the Guardian earlier this year that she “never wastes a word” in meetings, and last year warned that the government was still caught up in an opposition mindset and preoccupied with taking on the left instead of speaking to the concerns of the country more widely. She has also been a leading advocate for the UK to take a more pro-Palestinian stance, including on statehood. Ed Miliband Foreign secretary Miliband is a great survivor in the Labour party – almost as much so as Andy Burnham. A former cabinet minister under Gordon Brown, he led Labour to an election defeat in 2015 before serving in the shadow cabinet of Keir Starmer. He was a leading contender to be chancellor but there were fears his positioning on the soft left and as a firm defender of Britain’s net zero commitments would spook the financial markets. Having once made a speech pitching some companies as producers and others as predators, he is not a favourite politician of big business. Born in north London in a political family, his father was a Marxist academic. He studied PPE at Oxford and worked mostly in politics and research before joining his older brother, David, in the House of Commons as a Labour MP. They became the first brothers to both serve around the cabinet table since before the second world war, and their sibling relationship was put to the test when Ed beat David in the leadership contest to succeed Brown with the support of the trade union vote. As Labour leader, he led in the polls for many months against David Cameron and identified ahead of his time the public mood on the cost of living but was pilloried by some of the rightwing media as gaffe-prone and somewhat awkward. In Starmer’s cabinet he was viewed as one of the most successful cabinet ministers at driving through his priorities, including Great British Energy, and blocking more drilling in the North Sea – a policy expected to be partially overturned by Burnham. As foreign secretary, he will be expected to take international matters off Burnham’s hands as much as possible, take a more pro-Gaza stance and potentially set a path to restoring Britain’s aid commitments to 0.7% of national income. He replaces Yvette Cooper. Yvette Cooper Health secretary Among the Starmer-era big beasts who had been tipped for survival, Cooper is shifted from the Foreign Office to the vital domestic post of secretary of state for health and social care, the suffix especially important given Burnham’s stated mission to finally unlock the clogged adult care system. Cooper does, of course, have recent experience of a tricky UK-focused post, starting out in Starmer’s government as home secretary, and she held several posts under Tony Blair and Gordon Brown. While still in her 50s, Cooper is approaching Labour “grandee” status, having been an MP since 1997 and getting her first taste of ministerial life at the tail end of the 20th century. When Labour’s 2015 leadership campaign began she was initially viewed as a favourite, but finished third behind Corbyn and a certain Andy Burnham. The daughter of a trade union leader, Cooper is a near-political lifer, having begun her career with then-shadow chancellor John Smith, although she did spend a period as a journalist before entering parliament. For a decade of her time in parliament, Cooper was part of a political double act with her husband, Ed Balls, who lost his seat in 2015 and later became a broadcaster. Pat McFadden Work and pensions secretary Some continuity had been expected with the new ministerial slate and it makes sense for this to happen with a department that could be central to Burnham’s political fortunes. McFadden, one of the more experienced operators in government, will be tasked with continuing to pilot through some sort of reform to social security, with the intention of limiting the costs without cuts or conditions of the sort that prompted a major backbench rebellion for Starmer. An MP since 2005, McFadden was soon propelled into the Blair and Brown frontbenches, and spent the buildup to the 2024 election as Starmer’s national campaigns coordinator, going on to take the Cabinet Office post at the centre of policymaking. A trusted voice on even the trickiest morning broadcast round, the child of two Irish speakers from County Donegal transplanted to Paisley, McFadden trod the traditional political path of researcher and policy adviser before winning his Wolverhampton South East seat. Louise Haigh Chancellor of the duchy of Lancaster, first secretary of state and Cabinet Office minister Haigh became a linchpin of Burnham’s byelection operation, so it is no surprise she is now his chief organiser in government and effectively his deputy as first secretary of state. As the most senior Cabinet Office minister, she will coordinate work across the government and oversee any changes to Whitehall. It marks a return to cabinet for the 38-year-old Haigh, who was sacked by Keir Starmer as transport secretary for not having told him fully about a fraud conviction over a missing mobile phone when she was in her 20s. She grew up in Sheffield and studied politics and government at LSE, then Nottingham University, before working in parliament, for Unite and later at the insurer Aviva. It was during her time at that company where she got in hot water over the phone, having reported it stolen but failed to update the police when it was found. She pleaded guilty to fraud, but it did not stop her political career. Elected in 2015, she was one of 36 Labour MPs to nominate Jeremy Corbyn for the Labour leadership that year and was given a frontbench role when he made her a shadow Cabinet Office minister at age 28. The MP for Sheffield Heeley rose through the junior ministerial ranks and was one of a handful of Corbyn’s frontbench to be appointed to Keir Starmer’s first cabinet when he took over. She became the shadow Northern Ireland secretary. Known for an engaging communication style, she retained her frontbench role even as Starmer moved his party to the right. As transport secretary, she oversaw the nationalisation of railways legislation until she was returned to the backbenches. She replaces Darren Jones. Who’s out? Rachel Reeves Former chancellor Reeves had always wanted the job of chancellor and was proud to be the UK’s first female holder of the office. She had worked hard to instil ironclad fiscal discipline on Labour, sticking broadly to her self-imposed fiscal rules and enforcing spending curbs across departments. However, some in Labour blamed early missteps from her Treasury for Starmer’s downfall, starting with the cut to the winter fuel payment, but also the rise in national insurance for employers that her critics in business claim stymied the jobs market and growth. Reeves can, however, claim a record of having started to turn the economy around. She made a valiant effort to stay in her job but ultimately was judged too close to the Starmer project to stay in the Treasury. She is believed to have turned down another senior position. A former child chess champion and Oxford economics graduate, Reeves had also faced criticisms from some newspapers for having exaggerated her cre…

title: Andy Burnham vows to ‘bring back hope’ as he unveils his cabinet
summary: New prime minister says government must ‘do better’ to deliver change but promises prudent approach to economy
body: Andy Burnham has said his arrival in power will “bring back hope” to Britain as he launched his premiership with the surprise appointment of John Healey as chancellor and a promise to repair the long-term damage caused to communities during the Thatcher era. The new UK prime minister acknowledged people were “fed up” with politics as he addressed the country after taking over from Keir Starmer in Downing Street, saying the government needed to “do better” to deliver real change. In his speech outside No 10, he said the government would announce “some breathing space” on Tuesday for people regarding the cost of living, with plans to cut energy bills and bus fares expected as part of a fully funded package. Burnham later told reporters that he would look at “any flexibility” within the government’s existing fiscal rules to help borrow billions more to invest in infrastructure – but would take a “very prudent” approach to the economy. UK 10-year gilt prices dropped after his comments, with the markets already on the edge over the risks of higher borrowing and increased debt. But they had closed by the time that Healey, 66, regarded as a safe pair of hands, was announced. The former defence secretary, whose resignation pushed Starmer to the brink, emerged as the compromise candidate after Ed Miliband and Shabana Mahmood were tipped for the role. Miliband takes over as foreign secretary while Mahmood remains at the Home Office. In his first world leader call, Burnham spoke to Donald Trump – who had previously suggested he was just mayor of “some town” – inviting him to Manchester for next year’s G20 summit. He also called Ukraine’s Volodymyr Zelenskyy and European Union chief Ursula von der Leyen. Burnham’s arrival in Downing Street as the UK’s sixth prime minister in a decade, after Labour MPs brutally toppled Starmer from office, gives his party an opportunity for a second hearing with the public, but could also unleash yet more instability. Speaking without notes, and watched by his wife, Marie-France van Heel, he said: “I am acutely conscious that I am the sixth person in the last 10 years to walk up this street, the seventh prime minister since 2016, making this a moment for reflection and new resolution. “It requires my generation of politicians to raise our game and rise to the new challenge. Britain needs to show the world that we can regain our stability once again, and that is our challenge to make politics work, to make it work better. “I know people at home are fed up with politics. I hear you, and I want to be honest with you. We have not been good enough, and we need to be better. We will be.” He told the assembled crowd: “Let’s build a new national sense of unity, of common purpose, and positivity. Let us make this the moment when Britain starts to believe again, the moment we bring back hope.” After sacking a tranche of Starmer loyalists, including chancellor Rachel Reeves – who was understood to have turned down another job to return to the backbenches – and deputy prime minister David Lammy, Burnham spent Monday evening making his cabinet appointments. In another senior appointment, Louise Haigh, Burnham’s right-hand woman, was made first secretary of state and chancellor of the duchy of Lancaster, running a beefed-up Cabinet Office that will be the “political engine room” driving his agenda. Yvette Cooper will move from the Foreign Office to the Department of Health, where she will oversee Burnham’s plans to transform the broken social care system, while Pat McFadden stays at the Department for Work and Pensions. Angela Rayner, the former deputy prime minister who resigned after a row over stamp duty, was reappointed to her previous role as housing secretary. Burnham, who took over from Starmer as the leader of the Labour party on Friday, has put transforming the UK’s economic system at the heart of his plans, despite limited room for manoeuvre before the next election given his commitment to the fiscal rules. “We will make this moment a circuit breaker for Britain, bringing forward the biggest changes in the last 40 years.
