A new political model and a new economic model,” he told the crowd gathered in the Downing Street sunshine. “In the 1980s, Britain took some wrong turns. Political power was centralised, economic power privatised, large parts of the country deindustrialised and they still haven’t recovered. “Many feel as though they’re still in decline, and they don’t have the ability to turn things around. And that’s why we will change politics to make it more collaborative, more about problem-solving than point-scoring. And we will take power out of here and carry it into every postcode in the land so that they can do more.” In appointing Healey, a former Treasury minister under Gordon Brown, Burnham has put an experienced figure in charge of delivering on his vision for the UK economy, combining fiscal responsibility with a focus on reindustrialisation, devolution, the cost of living and growth. The new chancellor will now also be responsible for finding the money to raise military spending to 3% by 2030, and is expected to look closely at defence bonds, which he had argued for when at the Ministry of Defence but were rejected by the Treasury as they meant more borrowing. After his speech, Burnham told reporters he was planning to announce a fully funded cost of living support package including support for energy bills and bus fares this year, which could eventually include more radical plans such as freezing private sector rents. “We just want to do some things that people will feel, and feel quite quickly. It won’t solve everything, it won’t take all of the pressure off, but it just shows the direction of travel and that we’re serious about helping them out,” he said. He also made comments about borrowing more, within the fiscal rules, which he had mooted on the Makerfield campaign trail, but which nevertheless unsettled the markets when made from No 10. “I’ve said we’ll stick to the fiscal rules and by that I mean the existing fiscal rules and use, obviously, any flexibility within them,” he said. “So none of this is about taking risks with the economy. I’ve never done that in any role that I’ve had. I’ve always taken a very prudent approach.” In his speech, Burnham announced his first instruction in power would be to end rough sleeping, after a visit to homeless people in central London on Monday. He made a similar pledge when he became Greater Manchester mayor but oversaw four years of rising street homelessness. He also suggested he could cut income tax for lower earners in a future budget, potentially raising the tax-free personal allowance, which has been frozen for the last five years. But he refused to rule out bringing back the 50p top rate of tax to pay for it. Speaking before leaving No 10 for the final time, Starmer told the assembled crowd that “my work is done” of turning round the Labour party, but gave Burnham a final warning about foreign affairs. “We face a world now where our enemies will stop at nothing to divide us,” he said. The outgoing prime minister, who left handwritten notes for all Downing Street staff, added: “I go with good grace, I go with a smile and I go proud of everything that we have achieved.”

title: Suspect in custody named following explosion at FBI building in New York
summary: Three people reported injured after incendiary device allegedly thrown at building also housing ICE field office
body: Authorities in New York City are investigating an explosion on Monday morning outside an FBI building in lower Manhattan that reportedly injured three people. Investigators said a person who allegedly threw an incendiary device at the scene was taken into custody. The FBI later released the name in a press conference, identifying the suspect as Andrew Arrabaca. “Mr Arrabaca came armed with bad intentions,” said James Barnacle, the assistant director in charge of the FBI New York Field Office in a televised press conference. He then listed numerous weapons in his possession. The incident occurred at the bureau’s headquarters at 26 Federal Plaza, a building that the Reuters news agency reported also ⁠houses an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) field office. Video posted on social media by an independent journalist showed white smoke billowing from just outside the building, and a number of armed law enforcement personnel in uniform emerging quickly from a doorway. A more detailed clip on CNN’s website appears to show the moment the device exploded, and a person on the ground surrounded by police officers or ICE personnel. According to the New York news channel PIX11, three people were injured, two of them requiring hospital treatment. The outlet cited officials who said a fire appeared to have been started by fireworks that were “loaded into a wagon bearing a sign that read ‘ICE out of our streets’”. In a statement, the FBI said: “This morning, an individual deployed an incendiary device outside 26 Federal Plaza. The individual has been taken into custody, and the FBI New York joint terrorism taskforce is investigating the incident.” NBC New York reported that Arrabaca “may have emotional issues” – and that he had previously served in the US army and the national guard. The top US House Democrat, Hakeem Jeffries, who represents a New York district, praised the quick response of authorities. “I am grateful to law enforcement for their swift actions to stop an attack at 26 Federal Plaza this morning, for successfully apprehending the suspect and for keeping the people of New York City safe,” he said in a statement. “Acts of terror and violence have no place in our city and will never be tolerated. The individual responsible for this must be prosecuted to the fullest extent of the law.” Protests against ICE, and the Trump administration’s hardline approach to immigration, have proliferated in recent weeks and months as the number of deaths at the hands of federal immigration authorities has increased. Lorenzo Salgado Araujo and Joan Sebastián Durán Guerrero were killed during separate encounters earlier in July with officers attempting to conduct traffic stops in Texas and Maine. And on 13 July, Jesús Manuel Arenas-Silva, a 45-year-old Venezuelan man, became the 22nd person to die in ICE custody this year after he was found unresponsive at a detention center in Georgia. The Trump administration had appeared to step back from its policy of hardline immigration enforcement after the shooting deaths of two unarmed US citizens – Alex Pretti and Renee Good – by federal agents in Minneapolis in January. But the homeland security secretary, Markwayne Mullin, denied on Friday that traffic stops by ICE and border patrol agents would be suspended in response to the most recent fatal shootings. And he promised that a surge of immigration arrests would continue. “We’re turning up the heat on the streets. We’re out there working harder than we ever have,” he said at a news conference.
